g.text: WHICH PI, AND WHICH GPIOs

g.desc: WHICH PI, AND WHICH GPIOs

g.text: Drawn against the 40-pin J8 header, which is electrically and mechanically

g.desc: Drawn against the 40-pin J8 header, which is electrically and mechanically

g.text: identical across Raspberry Pi 2, 3, 4, 5 and Zero 2 W. Nothing on this sheet

g.desc: identical across Raspberry Pi 2, 3, 4, 5 and Zero 2 W. Nothing on this sheet

g.text: changes with the model.

g.desc: changes with the model.

g.text: The reference build is a RASPBERRY PI 3 MODEL B+ (BCM2837B0, Cortex-A53). What

g.desc: The reference build is a RASPBERRY PI 3 MODEL B+ (BCM2837B0, Cortex-A53). What

g.text: that pins down is firmware-side, not wiring-side - the target triple, the kernel

g.desc: that pins down is firmware-side, not wiring-side - the target triple, the kernel

g.text: image name, the ARM load address and the peripheral base - and it belongs to the

g.desc: image name, the ARM load address and the peripheral base - and it belongs to the

g.text: bare-metal boot story. This sheet does not constrain any of it.

g.desc: bare-metal boot story. This sheet does not constrain any of it.

g.text: WHY GPIO17 AND GPIO27, and not simply the first two free pins:

g.desc: WHY GPIO17 AND GPIO27, and not simply the first two free pins:

g.text: * Neither has a boot-time role. Nothing samples them at reset, and neither is

g.desc: * Neither has a boot-time role. Nothing samples them at reset, and neither is

g.text: used by the GPU or the firmware blobs.

g.desc: used by the GPU or the firmware blobs.

g.text: * Neither carries an alternate function this system needs. GPIO17's alternates

g.desc: * Neither carries an alternate function this system needs. GPIO17's alternates

g.text: are RTS0 and SPI1_CE1_N; GPIO27's are SD1_DAT3 and ARM_TMS. None is in play.

g.desc: are RTS0 and SPI1_CE1_N; GPIO27's are SD1_DAT3 and ARM_TMS. None is in play.

g.text: * Both default to a pull-DOWN, which is what makes the LED dark at power-on and

g.desc: * Both default to a pull-DOWN, which is what makes the LED dark at power-on and

g.text: what R5 is sized against for the button.

g.desc: what R5 is sized against for the button.

g.text: * They sit next to the UART pins used on sheet 6, so one harness reaches all four.

g.desc: * They sit next to the UART pins used on sheet 6, so one harness reaches all four.

g.text: AVOID GPIO0 and GPIO1, pins 27 and 28: they are the HAT ID EEPROM lines and are

g.desc: AVOID GPIO0 and GPIO1, pins 27 and 28: they are the HAT ID EEPROM lines and are

g.text: read at boot. AVOID GPIO2 and GPIO3, pins 3 and 5: they have fixed on-board

g.desc: read at boot. AVOID GPIO2 and GPIO3, pins 3 and 5: they have fixed on-board

g.text: pull-ups that cannot be turned off.

g.desc: pull-ups that cannot be turned off.

g.text: THE STATUS LED - AND WHY IT IS DARK BEFORE THE PI BOOTS

g.desc: THE STATUS LED - AND WHY IT IS DARK BEFORE THE PI BOOTS

g.text: D1 is active high: driving GPIO17 high lights it.

g.desc: D1 is active high: driving GPIO17 high lights it.

g.text: The Pi guarantees VOH >= 3.0 V at 2 mA on its default 8 mA drive strength, so 1.4 mA

g.desc: The Pi guarantees VOH >= 3.0 V at 2 mA on its default 8 mA drive strength, so 1.4 mA

g.text: sits well inside that and the real current is nearer 1.1 mA. Dim, and unmistakable

g.desc: sits well inside that and the real current is nearer 1.1 mA. Dim, and unmistakable

g.text: indoors. 330 ohm, about 4 mA, is the practical floor if you want it brighter - still

g.desc: indoors. 330 ohm, about 4 mA, is the practical floor if you want it brighter - still

g.text: half the default drive strength and a quarter of the 16 mA maximum. Only one pin

g.desc: half the default drive strength and a quarter of the 16 mA maximum. Only one pin

g.text: sources current on this board, so the aggregate GPIO budget is not a constraint here.

g.desc: sources current on this board, so the aggregate GPIO budget is not a constraint here.

g.text: It would be, with a bar of eight.

g.desc: It would be, with a bar of eight.

g.text: DARK BEFORE FIRMWARE RUNS, for free. The Raspberry Pi documentation: "All GPIO pins

g.desc: DARK BEFORE FIRMWARE RUNS, for free. The Raspberry Pi documentation: "All GPIO pins

g.text: revert to general-purpose inputs on power-on reset. The default pull states are also

g.desc: revert to general-purpose inputs on power-on reset. The default pull states are also

g.text: applied". GPIO17's default pull is Low. So at power-on the pin is an input held down

g.desc: applied". GPIO17's default pull is Low. So at power-on the pin is an input held down

g.text: by a 50-65 kohm pull-down, the LED sees leakage, and it is off.

g.desc: by a 50-65 kohm pull-down, the LED sees leakage, and it is off.

g.text: That is the same guarantee R2 buys for the light on sheet 3 - except here it comes

g.desc: That is the same guarantee R2 buys for the light on sheet 3 - except here it comes

g.text: free with the silicon's reset state, and there it had to be built out of a resistor

g.desc: free with the silicon's reset state, and there it had to be built out of a resistor

g.text: and defended against the SAM3X's reset state, which pulls the other way. Worth

g.desc: and defended against the SAM3X's reset state, which pulls the other way. Worth

g.text: noticing that the two boards default in opposite directions; it is the kind of thing

g.desc: noticing that the two boards default in opposite directions; it is the kind of thing

g.text: that is obvious on a diagram and invisible in code.

g.desc: that is obvious on a diagram and invisible in code.

g.text: THE BUTTON - AND THE INTERNAL PULL-DOWN IT HAS TO BEAT

g.desc: THE BUTTON - AND THE INTERNAL PULL-DOWN IT HAS TO BEAT

g.text: GPIO27 powers up as an input with a DEFAULT PULL-DOWN of 50-65 kohm. Both halves of

g.desc: GPIO27 powers up as an input with a DEFAULT PULL-DOWN of 50-65 kohm. Both halves of

g.text: that come from primary sources: BCM2835 ARM Peripherals table 6-31 lists GPIO9

g.desc: that come from primary sources: BCM2835 ARM Peripherals table 6-31 lists GPIO9

g.text: through GPIO27 as "Low", and the Raspberry Pi GPIO documentation gives RPD = 50-65

g.desc: through GPIO27 as "Low", and the Raspberry Pi GPIO documentation gives RPD = 50-65

g.text: kohm. Every GPIO on this header has some default pull, and firmware has not run yet

g.desc: kohm. Every GPIO on this header has some default pull, and firmware has not run yet

g.text: when you first apply power.

g.desc: when you first apply power.

g.text: R5 is therefore not a formality. It has to win a divider against a pull-down that is

g.desc: R5 is therefore not a formality. It has to win a divider against a pull-down that is

g.text: already there, at the worst corner - RPD at its 50 kohm minimum, where it is

g.desc: already there, at the worst corner - RPD at its 50 kohm minimum, where it is

g.text: strongest:

g.desc: strongest:

g.text: released node = 3.3 V x 50k / (4.7k + 50k) = 3.02 V

g.desc: released node = 3.3 V x 50k / (4.7k + 50k) = 3.02 V

g.text: against the Pi's thresholds of VIH min 1.6 V and VIL max 0.9 V: 1.4 V of margin

g.desc: against the Pi's thresholds of VIH min 1.6 V and VIL max 0.9 V: 1.4 V of margin

g.text: high, 0.6 V low. Active low - the same polarity as the Due's wall switch, on purpose.

g.desc: high, 0.6 V low. Active low - the same polarity as the Due's wall switch, on purpose.

g.text: PICK R5 = 47 kohm INSTEAD - a value that would be entirely sensible on a part with no

g.desc: PICK R5 = 47 kohm INSTEAD - a value that would be entirely sensible on a part with no

g.text: internal pull - and the released level becomes 3.3 x 50/97 = 1.70 V, which is 0.1 V

g.desc: internal pull - and the released level becomes 3.3 x 50/97 = 1.70 V, which is 0.1 V

g.text: above VIH min. It would work on the bench and fail at the other end of the tolerance

g.desc: above VIH min. It would work on the bench and fail at the other end of the tolerance

g.text: band, intermittently, in a way that looks like a debounce bug.

g.desc: band, intermittently, in a way that looks like a debounce bug.

g.text: R6 and C4 are the same network, for the same reasons, as the Due's wall switch on

g.desc: R6 and C4 are the same network, for the same reasons, as the Due's wall switch on

g.text: sheet 4: R6 caps the capacitor discharge through the contacts at 3.3 V / 470 ohm =

g.desc: sheet 4: R6 caps the capacitor discharge through the contacts at 3.3 V / 470 ohm =

g.text: 7.0 mA, and the pair filters sub-millisecond chatter into a monotonic edge. Time

g.desc: 7.0 mA, and the pair filters sub-millisecond chatter into a monotonic edge. Time

g.text: constants 0.43 ms rising, 43 us falling. The Pi's pads have selectable Schmitt-trigger

g.desc: constants 0.43 ms rising, 43 us falling. The Pi's pads have selectable Schmitt-trigger

g.text: input filtering, which firmware should leave enabled.

g.desc: input filtering, which firmware should leave enabled.

g.text: SW2 IS MOMENTARY. Unlike SW1 it has a rest state it returns to, and only its closing

g.desc: SW2 IS MOMENTARY. Unlike SW1 it has a rest state it returns to, and only its closing

g.text: edge is an event.

g.desc: edge is an event.

g.text: 3.3 V ONLY - AND PIN 2 OF THIS HEADER IS 5 V

g.desc: 3.3 V ONLY - AND PIN 2 OF THIS HEADER IS 5 V

g.text: Every GPIO on J8 is a 3.3 V pin and none of them is 5 V tolerant. From the

g.desc: Every GPIO on J8 is a 3.3 V pin and none of them is 5 V tolerant. From the

g.text: Raspberry Pi documentation: "The remaining pins are all general-purpose 3.3 V pins,

g.desc: Raspberry Pi documentation: "The remaining pins are all general-purpose 3.3 V pins,

g.text: meaning outputs are set to 3.3 V and inputs are 3.3V-tolerant", and, plainly,

g.desc: meaning outputs are set to 3.3 V and inputs are 3.3V-tolerant", and, plainly,

g.text: "Do not use 5V for 3.3V components."

g.desc: "Do not use 5V for 3.3V components."

g.text: There is no clamp that will save you. Over-voltage on a GPIO triggers latch-up in

g.desc: There is no clamp that will save you. Over-voltage on a GPIO triggers latch-up in

g.text: the pad driver, which shorts the 3V3 rail to ground inside the silicon. The board

g.desc: the pad driver, which shorts the 3V3 rail to ground inside the silicon. The board

g.text: does not recover.

g.desc: does not recover.

g.text: AND THE 5 V PINS ARE THE CLOSEST PINS ON THE HEADER:

g.desc: AND THE 5 V PINS ARE THE CLOSEST PINS ON THE HEADER:

g.text: pin 1 3V3 pin 2 5V <-- directly opposite 3V3

g.desc: pin 1 3V3 pin 2 5V <-- directly opposite 3V3

g.desc: pin 3 GPIO2 SDA1 pin 4 5V <-- and again

g.desc: pin 15 GPIO22 pin 16 GPIO23

g.text: Pin 1 is the square pad, at the end of the header nearest the micro-SD card. The

g.desc: Pin 1 is the square pad, at the end of the header nearest the micro-SD card. The

g.text: odd-numbered pins are one row and the even-numbered pins the other. Take 3V3 from

g.desc: odd-numbered pins are one row and the even-numbered pins the other. Take 3V3 from

g.text: pin 1 or 17 and GND from pin 6, 9 or 14.

g.desc: pin 1 or 17 and GND from pin 6, 9 or 14.

g.text: A one-position slip on the even row - pin 2 or pin 4 instead of the ground you

g.desc: A one-position slip on the even row - pin 2 or pin 4 instead of the ground you

g.text: meant - puts 5 V straight onto a 3.3 V rail. Confirm pin 1 against the silkscreen

g.desc: meant - puts 5 V straight onto a 3.3 V rail. Confirm pin 1 against the silkscreen
